issue_title: deisctl start platform hangs at `deis-store-monitor.service: activating/start-pre`
issue_body:
I've installed deis v1.6.1 on to my MacBook Pro (2.2 GHz Intel Core i7, 16 GB 1333 MHz DDR3) and followed the instructions for using vagrant. When I run `deisctl start platform` on an otherwise idle system, `deisctl` stops at `deis-store-monitor.service: activating/start-pre` for close to 30 minutes before moving on to `deis-store-monitor.service: active/running`.  

Thinking that giving the virtual machines more CPUs might help the situation, I made the following modifications to `Vagrantfile`

```bash
$ diff --git a/Vagrantfile b/Vagrantfile
index c0d9dad..becab9d 100644
--- a/Vagrantfile
+++ b/Vagrantfile
@@ -20,7 +20,7 @@ $enable_serial_logging = false
 $share_home = false

issue_title: X- prefix is deprecated in headers

issue_title: Add tests for `PipeDB` and corresponding process
issue_body:
### What is wrong?

#354 introduces a new database wrapper `trinity.db.pipe.PipeDB` which accesses a database behind a `multiprocessing.Pipe` connection.  There are no tests.

### How can it be fixed

Fill in a simple test suite against an in memory database.  We should spin up a new process in the tests running the `db_over_pipe` process and then test that we can interact with the database as expected using a `PipeDB` instance.

issue_title: Database architecture refactor: Adapters and Wrappers
issue_body:
Replaces https://github.com/ethereum/py-evm/issues/410

### What is wrong?

The current architecture uses a single global `ChainDB` for each chain.

This is proving problematic because:

- The `ChainDB` is not aware of different VM rules and thus cannot have it's underlying business logic modified.
- Some of the network syncing code needs to interact with the database in ways that aren't currently supported (can't recall exactly but it has to do with an assumption that transactions are available in the database).
- It has a lot of API surface area.

### How can it be fixed

First, we establish the following new terminology.

- *Connection*: The base database class used to connect to the underlying db.  e.g. `MemoryDB` or the `LevelDB`.  This object is treated as a key/value store.
- *Wrapper*: A class which *wraps* a *connection*, adding some extra functionality.  e.g. `JournalDB`, `HashTrie`
- *Adapter*: A class which implements a high level API over top of an underlying database.

We also establish a new convention/rule regarding the APIs exposed by *wrappers*.  Wrapper APIs may **only** be used at the same level that the wrapper is applied.

For Example:

Suppose the `VM` class applies the `JournalDB` wrapper to the database connection, and then passes the resulting object down to the `VMState`.  The `VMState` is not allowed to interact with any of the APIs exposed by the `JournalDB`.

Now, we establish the following new Adapter classes.

- `MetadataDB`: 
  - Only deals with chain metadata (e.g. canonical head, mapping block numbers to canonical blocks, mapping transaction hashes to canonical block in which it was mined).
  - Used by the `Chain` class.
- `ChainDB`:
  - Deals with storage of chain data (e.g. blocks, transactions, receipts)
  - Each `VM` may specify their own `ChainDB` class.
- `AccountDB`:
  - Replaces `evm.db.state.AccountStateDB`
  - Deals with VM state (e.g. account balances, nonces, storage, code)
  - each `VMState` may specify their own `AccountDB` class.

So, responsibility for databases is as follows.

- `Chain` -> `MetadataDB` (replaces what we currently refer to as `ChainDB`
- `VM` -> `ChainDB` (not to be confused with the existing `ChainDB`
- `VMState` -> `AccountDB` (replaces the current `AccountStateDB`

With this structure in place, now we explore how *wrappers* come into play.  Each level of the call stack will pass down it's base database which *might* be the actual base connection, or *might* be a wrapped version of the base connection.  Everything is to **assume** that the `db` object it receives **only** exposes the base database API.

In the event that a database connection needs to be wrapped, it should occur *at* the level at which the wrapper APIs will be used.  

For example: 

- the `AccountDB` should be responsible for applying the `HashTrie` wrapper.
- the `VMState` should be responsible for applying the `JournalDB` wrapper.

issue_title: Get readthedocs updating again
issue_body: It stopped updating with the latest commits, presumably when the repo location changed.

issue_title: SEM-I
issue_body:
Support a semantic-interface (SEM-I). This should do the following:

- [ ] read `semi.vpm` files
- [ ] execute variable type and property mapping forward and backward
- [ ] read `.smi` files for inspection

Additionally, it might be useful to use the `.smi` file for validating or normalizing predicates (e.g. re-adding `_rel` to short forms) or predications (instantiating ARGXs for dropped args)

issue_title: Code smell in moving files to trash
issue_body:
I ran across this while debugging a conda build issue: https://github.com/conda/conda/blob/master/conda/install.py#L964-L965

wherein we basically create a tmp folder, like

c:\users\frank\miniconda\conda-bld\tmp4326jklasdf

Then append

..\long\other\path\to\conda-bld

to it.  So we toss the random dir we just added.  I have no idea why we don't just use the random dir.  I'll put in a PR if you agree this doesn't make sense.  Otherwise, I'd like to be enlightened.

issue_title: Use conda to check for the installation integrity
issue_body:
Hi everybody,

I have a question. I installed anaconda to my laptop but for some unexpected reason, the computer restarted when the installation was running. I wonder if any tool to check for the package installation integrity when such incident happen. Reinstallation seems reasonable but I'm after an automated tool because I have installed additional packages so it took time.

Thanks for your suggestions.

Regards

issue_title: conda may critically depend on anaconda-client
issue_body:
I upgraded to conda 4.1.0rc1 to see i) test it out; and ii) see if it would resolve some conda clone issues I have been having ( @mcg1969 suggested that this release would do "clone" better, albeit differently).

RC1 sounded like it should be stable, and so I was feeling reasonably confident about updating it on a test system I have in place (Trial VM Phase 2). issue_title: conda vs conda env has too much confusion
issue_body:
Many issues have been brought up by customers using different methods we have put forward. This leads to confusion on when to use conda or conda env. 

Here are some examples of duplicate actions:

list available environments:

    conda info -e
    conda env list

The following has had failures with "conda env" and has been suggested to use the "--all" flag with conda instead, https://github.com/conda/conda/issues/3215. Two ways are confusing especially if one doesn't always work.

    conda env remove -n env_name
    conda remove -n env_name —-all

The docs suggest the following  to build identical environments, https://conda.io/docs/using/envs.html#build-identical-conda-environments

    conda list -n test —explicit > testexp.txt
    conda create -n test —file testexp.txt

But suggest sharing environments with the following, https://conda.io/docs/using/envs.html#share-an-environment

    conda env export -n env_name > environment.yml
    conda env create —file environment.yml

issue_title: conda create is too slow in Windows
issue_body:
Creating conda virtual python env in Windows is to slow compared to Linux.
For example, `conda create -n py34 python=3.4` with Windows note PC took 1-2 min while it only took a few sec with Ubuntu 14.04 (in VirtualBox for the same note PC).

Does `conda create` do different things in Windows and Linux?

issue_title: IPython terminal wrapping problems
issue_body:
When using Python 3.x -based conda environments I am experiencing problems with wrapping of long commands in the IPython terminal:

![screenshot_2016-06-21_15-58-08](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/2922144/16234306/fba8e056-37c8-11e6-8945-e6786693c6f1.png)

In this case I was trying to scroll back left along a previous command in order to edit it. The act of scrolling along the line triggered this observed behaviour where the line replicated several times vertically, garbling the line contents as it did so. As evidenced in the outputs I had to Ctrl-C to stop the vertical scrolling.

This happens with every command that I try to edit which exceeds the width of the window.

This behaviour does not occur when I am using my system's IPython with Python 2.7 (i.e. no conda environment active). 

I have previously tried opening a new terminal with a 'clean' .bashrc and adding conda to the PATH myself before activating the environment and this also causes problems so I don't think it's a PS1-related problem, at least not one that I've brought upon myself with a custom .bashrc.

issue_title: conda has channels not specified in .condarc file
issue_body:
`conda info` reports some channels from a localhost installation of Anaconda Repository including tokens (see below, token strings removed by me):

```
$ sudo -u guest /opt/wakari/anaconda/bin/conda info 
Using Anaconda Cloud api site http://localhost:8080/api
Current conda install:

             platform : linux-64
        conda version : 3.19.3
  conda-build version : 1.18.2
       python version : 2.7.11.final.0
     requests version : 2.9.1
     root environment : /opt/wakari/anaconda  (read only)
  default environment : /opt/wakari/anaconda
     envs directories : /home/guest/.conda/envs
                        /home/guest/envs
                        /opt/wakari/anaconda/envs
        package cache : /home/guest/.conda/envs/.pkgs
                        /home/guest/envs/.pkgs
                        /opt/wakari/anaconda/pkgs
         channel URLs : http://localhost:8080/conda/anaconda/linux-64/
                        http://localhost:8080/conda/anaconda/noarch/
                        https://conda.anaconda.org/wakari/linux-64/
                        https://conda.anaconda.org/wakari/noarch/
                        https://repo.continuum.io/pkgs/free/linux-64/
                        https://repo.continuum.io/pkgs/free/noarch/
                        https://repo.continuum.io/pkgs/pro/linux-64/
                        https://repo.continuum.io/pkgs/pro/noarch/
                        http://localhost:8080/conda/t/SOME_TOKEN/r/linux-64/
                        http://localhost:8080/conda/t/SOME_TOKEN/r/noarch/
          config file : /opt/wakari/anaconda/.condarc
    is foreign system : False
```

Those last two channel entries are a mystery to me, since here is the config file that `conda info` says is being used, and you can see that it comments out the lines with any tokens involved (and in any case they are to Anaconda Cloud, not to local host, and the 

```
$ cat /opt/wakari/anaconda/.condarc

channels:
  - http://localhost:8080/conda/anaconda
  - https://conda.anaconda.org/wakari
#  - https://conda.anaconda.org/t/DIFFERENT_TOKEN/anaconda-nb-extensions
  - defaults
  - r

create_default_packages:
  - anaconda-client
  - python
  - ipython-we
  - pip
  - r-essentials
```

Where else does conda get channel configuration information from?  If it gets it from anywhere else, why isn't that listed in the output of `conda info`?

issue_title: Non-standard, uppercase RG header record tags stripped silently.
Allow multiple arguments to read_callback function in count_coverage
issue_body:
I'm using the pysam function `count_coverage`, described [here](http://pysam.readthedocs.io/en/latest/api.html#pysam.AlignmentFile.count_coverage), to compute per-site read depth from a bamfile. Within `count_coverage`, I'm using `read_callback` to specify my own user-defined function for filtering out reads that fail to meet a series of defined conditions. However, the read_callback function can only take one argument, so all other arguments must be global variables. 

It would be great to be able to specify multiple arguments to the read_callback function, instead of using global variables. Is this possible?

This is what I currently have:
```
# read_callback function
def keep_read(aln):
   if np.mean(aln.query_qualities) < min_readq:
      return False
   elif aln.mapping_quality < min_mapq:
      return False
   else:
      return True

# global arguments to read_callback function
min_readq=20
min_mapq=30

# count coverage
with pysam.AlignmentFile(bampath, 'rb') as bamfile:
   counts = bamfile.count_coverage(chrom, start, end, read_callback=keep_read)
```

This is what I'd like:
```
# read_callback function
def keep_read(aln, min_readq, min_mapq):
   if np.mean(aln.query_qualities) < min_readq:
      return False
   elif aln.mapping_quality < min_mapq:
      return False
   else:
      return True

# count coverage
with pysam.AlignmentFile(bampath, 'rb') as bamfile:
   counts = bamfile.count_coverage(chrom, start, end, read_callback=keep_read, args=[20, 30])
```

Thanks,
Stephen

issue_title: Release GIL for calls to  htslib
issue_body:
See discussion here: https://groups.google.com/forum/#!topic/pysam-user-group/KNYbFdAKVX4

Calls to htslib do not release the GIL and potentially cause blocking in multi-threading applications where one thread waits from I/O from another thread.

issue_title: timestamp= parameter seems to have no effect
issue_body:
Regardless of the time I put in the timestamp= parameter or @timestamp field of the payload, when indexing a message, the timestamp that gets placed on that message always seems to be the timestamp of when the data was indexed, as opposed to the date of the value in the timestamp= field or @timestamp within the payload! 

This breaks queuing logic that may be indexing messages that built up during an outage for example. When the outage is restored and the queue is emptied, all messages from within the queue get indexed on top of each other in terms of the timestamp due to this bug.

issue_title: Incorrect tab completion on 'appservice plan create' name parameter
issue_body:
There is tab completion on the name of an appservice plan on create when there shouldn't be.

```
$ az appservice plan create -g debekoe-1 -n [tab][tab]
debekoe-plan1  debekoe-plan  
```

There shouldn't be tab completion here as it's a create command and the name has to be a name of a plan that doesn't already exist.

issue_title: push/pop for az configure defaults

issue_title: [DNS] Removing last record in record set should remove the record set
issue_body:
Suppose I add a record
`az network dns record a add -g rg1 -z jt-test.com --record-set-name cli2 --ipv4-address 1.2.3.5`

Then delete it again
`az network dns record a remove -g rg1 -z jt-test.com --record-set-name cli2 --ipv4-address 1.2.3.5`

I should, by default, be back where I started.  But I'm not...currently, 'remove' is removing the record but leaving behind an empty record set.

Instead, when removing the last record from a record set, *by default* the record set should also be removed.

The 'remove' commands (for each record type) should support a parameter '--keep-empty-record-set' to override this behaviour and keep the empty record set if so desired.

The help text must also be update to make this clear.

issue_title: network: support cross tenant vnet peering
issue_body:
In a nutshell, the request should include `x-ms-authorization-auxiliary` header with tokens to access the external tenant which manages the subscription where the external vnet resides
For now, vnet peering is the only service feature requiring cross tenant support, though potentially we might have more coming up say cross tenant image sharing, but we don't know yet.

issue_title: Replace isodate
issue_body:
Following discussion with @zooba about [isodate](https://github.com/gweis/isodate):

- Isodate is not maintained anymore for 2 years. Direct Gmail email to the author get no answer as well.
- Isodate still uses 2to3
- [I got no issue adapting it without 2to3](https://github.com/lmazuel/isodate), but since the owner abandoned the project, it's useless.

The features we use of isodate are:
- Parsing / serializing of ISO8601 datetime
- Parsing / serializing of ISO8601 duration

Datetime is simple, and move from isodate is straightforward.

Duration is way more complicated, since this is **relative** duration (one month can't be converted to `timedelta`). Isodate algorithm returns `timedelta` instances for duration defined in days/hours/..., and a specific `isodate.Duration` class for ambiguous relative duration. It's dynamic based on what is parsed.

I found [aniso8601](https://pypi.python.org/pypi/aniso8601/) to parse duration. Aniso8601 is not using dynamic return type, there is a `relative` flag to decide if you want `timedelta` (might be converted with 1 month == 30 days), or `dateutil.relativedelta` to keep the precision. Aniso8601 does not serialize, but it's not the most complicated part.

Moving this dependency will also imply that users will get different object from a SDK call, moving from the couple `timedelta`/`isodate.Duration` to `dateutil.relativedelta`.

Considering that the issue itself (2to3 on Py3 install) is not that critical for now, and that there is no direct obvious solution, I plan to do nothing for now, but I keep this issue open for the record of what I found. Eventually we'll have to work on this.

FYI @johanste

issue_title: MSIauthentication returns underscore_separated keys in token data, unlike adal.
issue_body:
Adal serializes the payload from a token request from under_scored to camelCased, whereas the MSIAuth in the msrestazure tools does not.

The same serialization present in adal being done for MSI would be ideal for consistency and make it easier for client python tools that use both auth flows.

issue_title: dry-rest-permissions doesn't support generic view
issue_body:
When I have a generic view like this one:

```python
class MemberDetailAPI(RetrieveUpdateAPIView):
    """
    Profile resource to display or update details of a member.
    """

    queryset = Profile.objects.all()
    lookup_field = 'user__id'
    obj_key_func = DetailKeyConstructor()
    permission_classes = (DRYPermissions,)

    @etag(obj_key_func)
    @cache_response(key_func=obj_key_func)
    def get(self, request, *args, **kwargs):
        profile = self.get_object()
        serializer = self.get_serializer(profile,
                                         show_email=profile.show_email,
                                         is_authenticated=self.request.user.is_authenticated())
        return Response(serializer.data)

    @etag(obj_key_func, rebuild_after_method_evaluation=True)
    def put(self, request, *args, **kwargs):
        self.permission_classes = (IsAuthenticatedOrReadOnly, IsOwnerOrReadOnly)

issue_title: BulletIK
issue_body:
**Problem 1: Parsing URDF**
It seems currently the Bullet IK can only parse arm urdf’s; that is, with the full robot model, it generates wrong IK solutions. The complete Sawyer robot model for example, has 20 joints, where 7 are activated joints and 13 are fixed joints. Bullet cannot construct a correct arm model from the full urdf, and the IK solutions are incorrect.

**Problem 2: Inverse Kinematics**
The robot with different joint states have different solutions for the same target pose. That is, the IK solutions are not state independent. 

It also needs multiple iterations to solve for one accurate solution. In bullet3/examples/pybullet/examples/inverse_kinematics.py, change use_real_time = 1 to 0, then the robot cannot reach to desired position at all.

The null space solution is very off. In bullet3/examples/pybullet/examples/inverse_kinematics.py, change use_null_space = 0 to 1, the actual and target lines are separated far away.

**Problem3: Rendering**
Depending on types of different hardware, the rendered color of the grippers are different. For example, a PR2 gripper image has red and green fingers, which is inconsistent with original yellow and green colors. This detriments the learning models based on visual inputs.

Pictures are attached in:
https://docs.google.com/document/d/1XH7GGaxPjahu-3z51t7aSw39DWgmjQ-NeFSvvyZYTdU/edit?usp=sharing

issue_title: Known, Time-Varying Emission Probabilities
issue_body:
I'm working in a simple setting where the means of the (Gaussian) emission distributions of the states are known beforehand, but change over time. The particular application is eye-tracking data, where I assume the subject is looking at one of several objects (and their eye-tracking data are normally distributed around the object), and would like to infer the object at which the subject is looking at each point in time, but the objects are _moving over time_.

My question is whether there is a simple way of using hmmlearn with these (known) time-varying means? I couldn't find any information about non-constant emission distributions.

If there were only one state, I could normalize the data by subtracting the mean of the emission distribution at each time point, but this mean is different for different states, so I don't think this kind of approach works.

For now, I can make reasonable guesses for the model parameters (the variance of the emission distributions, the starting probabilities and the transition matrix), but, eventually, I would also like the transition matrix to vary over time (e.g., the transition probability between two objects is probably a function of the distance between the two objects).

issue_title: auto_decompress=False modified binary data somehow
issue_body:
## Long story short

Use aiohttp with `auto_decompress=False` and try to download binary data. Here is a Python snippet example also in [this gist](https://gist.github.com/bmbouter/8665d36b1175ce8176ce63e7b403a906).

```
import asyncio
import aiohttp

async def my_download():
    with open('/tmp/somefileintmp', 'wb') as the_file:
        async with aiohttp.ClientSession(auto_decompress=False) as session:
            async with session.get('https://repos.fedorapeople.org/pulp/pulp/fixtures/python-pypi/packages/shelf_reader-0.1-py2-none-any.whl') as resp:
                while True:
                    chunk = await resp.content.read(1024 * 1024)
                    if not chunk:
                        break  # the download is done
                    the_file.write(chunk)

loop = asyncio.get_event_loop()
loop.run_until_complete(my_download())
loop.close()
```

Now let's download it with wget and do some sha256 comparisons. Run these shell commmands:

```
$ sha256sum /tmp/somefileintmp 
ecaca570978364bcec8e0a4c2796361aecd6600c0d9f949cefba3b2afbdf4141  /tmp/somefileintmp
$ wget https://repos.fedorapeople.org/pulp/pulp/fixtures/python-pypi/packages/shelf_reader-0.1-py2-none-any.whl
$ sha256sum shelf_reader-0.1-py2-none-any.whl
2eceb1643c10c5e4a65970baf63bde43b79cbdac7de81dae853ce47ab05197e9  shelf_reader-0.1-py2-none-any.whl
```

## Expected behaviour

I expect when `auto_decompress=False` for the data to be read without alteration.

## Actual behaviour

The data is being transformed into a gzip file. At least that is what the `file` utility says. When I run file on both downloaded version I get:

```
$ file /tmp/somefileintmp 
/tmp/somefileintmp: gzip compressed data, from Unix
$ file ~/shelf_reader-0.1-py2-none-any.whl 
/home/vagrant/shelf_reader-0.1-py2-none-any.whl: Zip archive data, at least v2.0 to extract
```

## Steps to reproduce

Run the scripts above in a Python 3.5 environment.

## Your environment

I'm using aiohttp v3.3.2 as a client.

issue_title: Parallel printing to stderr
issue_body:
Hello, I am using joblib.Parallel to parallelize my computations. Parallel is able to print out to stderr the progress of the calculations. This works fine in IPython console where the progress is updated every few seconds, but in jupyter notebook the progress is only printed once the computation has completed.

If you want to try it:

```python
import time
from math import sqrt
import joblib

def my_sqrt(x):
    time.sleep(0.1)
    return sqrt(x)

joblib.Parallel(n_jobs=2, verbose=11)(joblib.delayed(my_sqrt)(i**2) for i in range(50))
```

I talked with the joblib devs and they said that it must be a problem in the Notebook since stderr should be printed directly.
It works fine in the Notebook as well if you put n_jobs=1 which just runs it serially so it seems like Notebook doesn't update stderr when there is threading.

I would appreciate any insight :)

issue_title: suggestion: name revocation and name reset
issue_body:
If a name gets either lost or stolen (by either the private key owner being lost or the private key being compromised), it's important to convey to the world that the name is no longer under the control of the owner.

If we could have a mechanism to remove a compromised name from the system (a flavor/variant of key revocation), then we could prevent the impersonation of websites, user identities, and certificates upon name theft.

More on key revocation, for reference: http://www.ac.uk.pgp.net/pgpnet/secemail/q4/node19.html

In order to make this work, we would need to do the following:
1. add a name operation for revoking names that can only be executed by the owner of the name
2. recommend that as soon as someone registers a name, they create and sign an anyone-can-pay transaction that revokes the name, then hide the signed transaction somewhere safe, in a separate location from the key (a third party could help out with this)
3. recommend the following: as soon as a name is stolen, the pre-signed name revocation transaction should be recovered and broadcasted
4. recommend the following: if a private key owner is compromised but the name hasn't yet been stolen, transfer the name to a new private key owner

This system closely parallels the key revocation system that PGP and other crypto identity systems have implemented, with a few variations to make it work with names as property owned by private keys.

issue_title: Why—oh god why—are we passing ints as strings?
issue_body:
While fixing #184, I found that we were calling `str(multiprocessing.cpu_count())`, which I thought was bizarre. It turns out that this is an optimization, because we wind up passing this `cpu_count` to the GATK as a core count, and since that call is going through `docker_call`/`check_call`, etc., it needs to be a string, and so on.

But, upon further reflection here and in #176, it turns out that this is sort of why we're getting OOD errors in the GATK pipelines. Since the cores argument is never set on any of the toil jobs in the GATK germline/preprocessing pipelines, the parameter defaults to 1 core. This leads to the occasional instance where multiple GATKs get put on a single node, which leads to good times, etc. You don't want to blanket set the core count (or disk requirements) because there are various parts of the pipeline that are single threaded, only operate on a reference file, etc.

So, while passing ints as strings is not always wrong, in this case, it was hiding a job resource request bug which led to tons of funny business.

issue_title: Remove "null" from fields and entities
issue_body:
The base `Field` class has the following signature:

```python
    def __init__(
            self,
            required=False,
            choices=None,
            default=_SENTINEL,
            null=False):
```

The "required", "choices" and "default" attributes are all used when generating random data for testing purposes. However, the "null" attribute is not used at all, anywhere, to the best of my knowledge. This is silly. Why should a completely useless attribute be bumming around?

Drop the `null` attribute. Doing so will not affect anything right now, given that there is no usage of the field. It should be done before version 1.0 is released, as dropping the attribute is a backward-incomptable change. (Some client may reference the attribute.) In addition, the attribute can be added again after version 1.0 is released without breaking anything - only prompting a minor version number bump.

issue_title: Exemple to Mesa: Animate the output
issue_body:
I don't now if i post right.

After basic tutorial you had : 
for model.py :
`
# model.py
from mesa.space import MultiGrid
from mesa import Agent, Model
from mesa.time import RandomActivation
import random

class MoneyModel(Model):
    """A model with some number of agents."""
    def __init__(self, N, width, height):
        self.num_agents = N
        self.grid = MultiGrid(width, height, True)
        self.schedule = RandomActivation(self)
        # Create agents
        for i in range(self.num_agents):
            a = MoneyAgent(i, self)
            self.schedule.add(a)
            # Add the agent to a random grid cell
            x = random.randrange(self.grid.width)
            y = random.randrange(self.grid.height)
            self.grid.place_agent(a, (x, y))

    def step(self):
        self.schedule.step()

class MoneyAgent(Agent):
    """ An agent with fixed initial wealth."""
    def __init__(self, unique_id, model):
        super().__init__(unique_id, model)
        self.wealth = 1

    def move(self):
        possible_steps = self.model.grid.get_neighborhood(
            self.pos,
            moore=True,
            include_center=False)
        new_position = random.choice(possible_steps)
        self.model.grid.move_agent(self, new_position)

    def give_money(self):
        cellmates = self.model.grid.get_cell_list_contents([self.pos])
        if len(cellmates) > 1:
            other = random.choice(cellmates)
            other.wealth += 1
            self.wealth -= 1

    def step(self):
        self.move()
        if self.wealth > 0:
            self.give_money()
`
And for run.py
`# run.py
from model import MoneyModel
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt


model = MoneyModel(50, 10, 10)
for i in range(20):
    model.step()

agent_counts = np.zeros((model.grid.width, model.grid.height))
for cell in model.grid.coord_iter():
    cell_content, x, y = cell
    agent_count = len(cell_content)
    agent_counts[x][y] = agent_count
plt.imshow(agent_counts, interpolation='nearest')
plt.colorbar()

# If running from a text editor or IDE, remember you'll need the following:
plt.show()
`
Now we will made this new file : 
`
# animator.py
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import matplotlib.animation as animation


def animator(data):
	fig = plt.figure()
	ims = []
	for add in data:
		ims.append((plt.pcolor(add),))
	im_ani = animation.ArtistAnimation(fig, ims, interval=100, repeat_delay=300, blit=True)
        # If you want save the animation
	# im_ani.save('im.mp4')
	plt.show()
`
--------=========--------

Now we need to change the run.py file to : 
`# run.py
from model import MoneyModel
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from animator import animator

frame = []

model = MoneyModel(50, 10, 10)
for i in range(20):
	model.step()
	agent_counts = np.zeros((model.grid.width, model.grid.height))
	for cell in model.grid.coord_iter():
		cell_content, x, y = cell
		agent_count = len(cell_content)
		agent_counts[x][y] = agent_count
	frame.append(agent_counts)


animator(frame)
`
Now you had a easy way to see a video, from each step of your model.

issue_title: add websocket urls endpoint to asm policy
add missing modify tests for iFiles
Optional parallel evaluation with fmin
issue_body:
There are cases where the objective cannot be computed in absolute terms, but only comparisons (relative `f`-values) are available. In this use case currently only the `ask`-and-`tell` interface is applicable.

`fmin` features noise handling and rejection sampling, which both rely on single evaluations (for rejection sampling it seems unavoidable). When these are not needed, a parallel evaluation option in the spirit of ``fmin(objective_fct, ..., parallelf=...)`` would be nice to have.

Proposal: the `objective_fct` may signal its parallel capability via an `eval_parallel` attribute like ``parallelf = getattr(objective_fct, 'eval_parallel', None)``.

Advantage: no additional arguments to `fmin`.

Disadvantage: slightly more difficult to spot in the docs than the additional argument to `fmin`. It is still the first entry in the parameter description which would describe this interface.

Usecase:
```python
class Objective: 
    eval_parallel = my_parallel_objective_function
xopt, es = cma.fmin2(Objective, ...)  # class or instance could be used here
```

Question: should single evaluations still be tried in this case (e.g. for the initial point, rejection sampling) and should there be a warning?

We may also consider a callback which is called only in the end of `tell`, though this may rarely be necessary.

issue_title: Multiple renewals with standalone don't work
issue_body:
If you create two separate lineages with the standalone configurator, and then try to renew both with the new `letsencrypt renew`, the second one will fail because the port is already in use.

The standalone plugin does try to stop the listening server when the challenge has been satisfied, but it seems that this fails to release the port. We may need to find a way to explicitly set `SO_REUSEADDR` on the listening socket (before it's bound!) in order to allow a listening socket to be immediately rebound.

issue_title: Producer: different callback per message
issue_body:
Version: librdkafka 0.9.1 and last pip confluent lib

```python

from confluent_kafka import Producer

p = Producer({'bootstrap.servers':'kafkahost'})

def callback(err,msg,k):
  print "callback received k: %s and v: %s" % (k,msg.value())

data = {
  'k1':'v1',
  'k2':'v2',
  'k3':'v3',
}

for k,v in data.iteritems():
  print "sending k: %s" % k
  p.produce('patricktest', v, callback=lambda err,msg: callback(err,msg,k))

p.flush()
```

The result is:
```
Mac:~$ python producertest.py
sending k: k3
sending k: k2
sending k: k1
callback received k: k1 and v: v3
callback received k: k1 and v: v2
callback received k: k1 and v: v1
```

and I would expect in the three last lines to be k3 k2 k1 rather than three times k1

After looking at the source code, it seems that each time a callback is defined, it's defined as a global parameter for the whole producer, rather than per message.

This breaks the general expectation that if you give a callback to a function/method, it's THIS function which will be called. I didn't find a workaround either to give arbitrary extra data to the callback - which was what I attempted via this intermediate lambda.

--Thanks

issue_title: CFLAGS and CPPFLAGS are not propagated
issue_body:
The standard `CFLAGS` are not propagated in the Makefile, but overwritten in every subdirectory. This has a few disadvantages: First, a local change (like the include dir for wcstools, which is `/usr/include/wcstools` on Debian) has to be set on each fo the zillion subdirs, and it is impossible to set compilation options from the environment. The latter is used in Debian to provide different build options (like with or without debugging), and to enable [specific hardening flags](https://wiki.debian.org/HardeningWalkthrough) and warnings/errors (`fstack-protector-strong -Wformat -Werror=format-security `).

Also, the standard `CPPFLAGS` and `LDFLAGS` are not recognized. They are also used for hardening in Debian (`-D_FORTIFY_SOURCE=2`).

Would it be possible to recognize `CFLAGS`, `CPPFLAGS`, and `LDFLAGS` from the build environment?

issue_title: Wrong update handling
issue_body:
Hello.

I found some strange thing: https://github.com/LonamiWebs/Telethon/blob/86d45cc27675df5d43928961bb1fb1e44e6db486/telethon/network/mtproto_sender.py#L120. You get list of updates, but only first elements passed to updates thread. Other updates are lost. It is not right.

Good solution is return list of updates and iterate it in update thread. Otherwise you will get random update lost from e.g. supergroups.

```python
def get_dialog(dialog_id, accept='application/wds+json'):

        auth = HTTPBasicAuth(config.username, config.password)

```

issue_title: Bug in normalizing ident var that is written as delins
issue_body:
When I was normalizing the clinvar variants, I found the variant

> NC_000003.12:g.46709584_46709610del27insAAGAAGAAGAAGAAGAAGAAGAAGAAG

which reference sequence is in fact the same as the alt sequence. So this is an ident variant. But current normalizer treats this variant to be delins and shuffles the var till the chromosome end.

issue_title: Powerline statusline loses its colours in Vim when `:w` is used
issue_body:
`vim --version` output:
```
VIM - Vi IMproved 7.4 (2013 Aug 10, compiled Nov 24 2016 16:44:48)
Included patches: 1-1689
Extra patches: 8.0.0056
```
Using the powerline theme for vim, here is how the theme is called:
```
set rtp+=$HOME/.local/lib/python2.7/site-packages/powerline/bindings/vim/
set laststatus=2
```

Each time I use `:w` to write to a file, my powerline statusline goes from looking like this:
![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/29816026/28464049-ddabd66e-6e66-11e7-89af-9efa9a9713fc.png)

...to looking like this: 
![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/29816026/28464067-f9577080-6e66-11e7-96f3-c037ffa8232f.png)

If I use `so %` to re-source the .vimrc file in the same session, the statusline colours return as normal (until `:w` is used again)

Doing some research, if I use `:verbose set statusline?`, it outputs `statusline=%!py3eval('powerline.statusline(1)')`. Seems like somewhere the powerline theme is calling to set the `statusline` param to `1`, as opposed to the value of `2` like it should be to have it persist. 

I realise this may not be a powerline theme issue, so please close if necessary (I have also posted this in the Vim issues thread)

issue_title: vmprofshow errors when run
issue_body:
Hi! I'm running pypy 5.1.2, and vmprof (0.4.6.dev1)
When I run
```
python -m vmprof -o profile.log solve.py
```
I get the error:
```
(pypy) root@ff95d4f3a727:~/app/testing3# vmprofshow profile.log
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/root/.virtualenvs/pypy/bin/vmprofshow", line 11, in <module>
    sys.exit(main())
  File "/root/.virtualenvs/pypy/site-packages/vmprof/show.py", line 263, in main
    pp.show(args.profile)
  File "/root/.virtualenvs/pypy/site-packages/vmprof/show.py", line 40, in show
    if stats.get_runtime_in_microseconds() < 1000000:
  File "/root/.virtualenvs/pypy/site-packages/vmprof/stats.py", line 33, in get_runtime_in_microseconds
    ts = self.end_time - self.start_time
TypeError: unsupported operand type(s) for -: 'NoneType' and 'NoneType'
```

The actual failure is on in [stats.py:33](https://github.com/vmprof/vmprof-python/blob/56a71e1dfb98b92caccac0620ae26e77c9d8b579/vmprof/stats.py#L33) but I found that isn't really the problem. Rather it's that the LogReaderState() function [reader.py:304](https://github.com/vmprof/vmprof-python/blob/56a71e1dfb98b92caccac0620ae26e77c9d8b579/vmprof/reader.py#L309-L310) returns a state that does not have a start or end time, which is supposed to be populated by the [read_all()](https://github.com/vmprof/vmprof-python/blob/56a71e1dfb98b92caccac0620ae26e77c9d8b579/vmprof/reader.py#L269) function, but for some reason mine is not doing that. I know that it's looking for a MARKER_TIME_N_ZONE in my log file which is '0x06' but I'm not sure where in the file it's expecting that to be so I'm not sure how to diagnose this further. Any help?

issue_body:

I want to serialize and deserialize it to/from a dictionary that has a key called "timecode" with the string representation. I created a field called "display" with an attribute of "load_from=timecode" and "dump_to=timecode" which works great, except when I use the "only attribute" (e.g. I want the serialized version to only contain the timecode string). Does it make sense for `only` to consider the field to exist if it can be pulled from the "load_from" value? Is there a better way to do what I'm trying to do?

issue_title: Reception Error - loosing data with bytearray
issue_body:
Dears,

I'm facing an problem using the pySerial module, a simple program that writes a data to the serial port and reads it back by an external hardware loopback is losing data.

Hardware :
 - Arduino DUE as UART FTDI (**loopback : Tx0 -> Rx0**)
 - Analog Discovery 2 ( debugging Tx0)

Software : 
 - Linux Ubuntu 16.04 
 - python3
 - pyserial==3.3

python code used on this simple loopback : 

```python
#!/usr/bin/env python3

import serial
import time

def main():
    print ("Testing Serial comunication ")

    name     = "/dev/ttyACM0"
    baudrate = 115200
    bytesize = serial.EIGHTBITS
    parity   = serial.PARITY_NONE
    stop     = serial.STOPBITS_ONE
    xonxoff  = serial.XOFF
    timeout  = 2.0

    port = serial.Serial(name,
                         baudrate,
                         bytesize,
                         parity,
                         stop,
                         timeout,
                         xonxoff)

    if not port is None:
        print (port.name)
        txBuffer = bytes(bytearray(range(24)))
        print("Tx buffer size : {}".format(len(txBuffer)))
        while (1) :
            txSize = port.write(txBuffer)
            rxBuffer = port.read(txSize)
            print("Tx trans size : {}".format(txSize))
            print("Rx trans size : {}".format(len(rxBuffer)))
            print(rxBuffer)
            time.sleep(0.1)


    else:
        print("Falha ao abrir a porta")


if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()
```

As result I get : 

![selecao_042](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/1039615/25027727/4e6663ae-2085-11e7-9cd0-da207b94f593.png)

If we check there is a loose on 2 bytes : **\x11** and **\x13**

But if we analyse the data on the UART the **\x11** and **\x13** are there :

issue_title: Improve documentation of `about gen`
issue_body:
Myself and others (https://github.com/nexB/aboutcode-manager/issues/238) have run into some confusion when trying to generate ABOUT files from an inventory file. 

Specifically, the default `MAPPING.CONFIG` values and file location were a mystery to me until consulting with @DennisClark. 

We should probably add some more documentation on both:
* what `about gen` defaults to when NOT passing `--mapping`
* the default location that AboutCode looks for the `MAPPING.CONFIG` file

https://github.com/nexB/aboutcode-toolkit/issues/305 Will go a long way to making this more clear as well.

issue_title: Python DataSet of ImageFrame needs to support predict, evaluation or have API to get ImageFrame
issue_body: Python DataSet of ImageFrame can not do prediction or evaluation. Either provide APIs to do prediction/evaluation, or provide API to get ImageFrame from Dataset, then use imageFrame to do prediction/evaluation. Also, ImageFrame does not have API to do evaluation, we may need to provide this API or provide API to extract Sample RDD from imageframe to do evaluation.

issue_title: ReadOnly fields are not rendered if their value is None

issue_title: Heartbeat problems when running with caffe
issue_body:
Background: I'm running my experiments using Caffe.
I had this weird error that my experiments would not terminate and heartbeat signals got lost without notice. It seems that this is due to the main thread accessing C++ stuff through boost.python bindings and that the heartbeat thread was simply not responding.

I was able to fix the problem by changing the IntervalTimer class
https://github.com/IDSIA/sacred/blob/d71cf7c6294e8b7d0c2a3992bfdf1eaf4cf00652/sacred/utils.py#L443
to inherit from `multiprocessing.Process` instead of `threading.Thread`.

If there's interest for adding this I can add a PR. This change should not break any existing functionality and allow for running experiments which use the boost.python interface.

EDIT: Just noticed that passing objects between processes is not as easy as I thought. I'll have to work out some more magic to make this work

issue_title: Option to disable exit(1) for failures
issue_body:
I've been using sacred quite a bit to run queued experiments in parallel (with the help of a script based on mongoassistant). It's been working great. It looks like commit 6c1c302 would cause problems for this setup though, because it causes sacred to call exit(1) on failure.

With the assistant approach, if an experiment fails the assistant script needs to continue running to cleanup and potentially move on to the next experiment. For example, the assistant script notifies me and exits if the error can't be handled, but it continues running the next queued experiment if the error can be handled. Calling exit(1) prevents this.

Do you have any suggestions on how to deal with this situation? For example, would making the exit(1) call optional make sense?

issue_title: Gromacs 5.x energy minimization not falling back to mdrun from mdrun_d
issue_body:
in `gromacs.setup.energy_minimize` the algorithm to determine which mdrun binary to use is:

```python
try:
    gromacs.mdrun_d(**mdrun_args)
except (AttributeError, OSError):
    # fall back to mdrun if no double precision binary
    ### Some warning warnings
    gromacs.mdrun(**mdrun_args)
```
This should, in practice invoke `gmx mdrun <options>` instead of `gmx mdrun_d <options>` (which should actually be `gmx_d mdrun`). I think that this worked in gromacs 4.x because the binary was just `mdrun` or `mdrun_d`. So, if `mdrun_d` was not found `mdrun` would take over. Since the binary being run is now just `gmx <subcommands>`, no errors occur. 

#### Relevant Tests 
Using a previously solvated system and topology.
When using the code as it was written above, I ran:
```python
import gromacs.setup
gromacs.setup.energy_minimize(struct='solvated.gro', mdp='em.mdp')
```
Which returned:
```

  gmx mdrun_d -c em.pdb -deffnm em -stepout 10 -v

-------------------------------------------------------

Source file: src/gromacs/commandline/cmdlinemodulemanager.cpp (line 376)
Function:    gmx::CommandLineModuleInterface* gmx::CommandLineModuleManager::Impl::processCommonOptions(gmx::CommandLineCommonOptionsHolder*, int*, char***)

Error in user input:
'mdrun_d' is not a GROMACS command.

For more information and tips for troubleshooting, please check the GROMACS
website at http://www.gromacs.org/Documentation/Errors
-------------------------------------------------------
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "test.py", line 3, in <module>
    gromacs.setup.energy_minimize(struct='solvated.gro',mdp='em.mdp')
  File "/home/ikenney/Library/python/GromacsWrapper/gromacs/setup.py", line 684, in energy_minimize
    gromacs.mdrun_d(**mdrun_args)
  File "/home/ikenney/Library/python/GromacsWrapper/gromacs/core.py", line 382, in __call__
    return self.run(*args,**kwargs)
  File "/home/ikenney/Library/python/GromacsWrapper/gromacs/core.py", line 160, in run
    results, p = self._run_command(*_args, **_kwargs)
  File "/home/ikenney/Library/python/GromacsWrapper/gromacs/core.py", line 587, in _run_command
    self.check_failure(result, command_string=p.command_string)

Command invocation: gmx mdrun_d -c em.pdb -deffnm em -stepout 10 -v
```
**However**, when I replace `mdrun_d` with `mdrun` in the `try` statement, mdrun _is_ invoked immediately, runs fine, and produces a working structure.

issue_title: fp.rem should produce NaN for 0/0
issue_body:
For the following benchmark:

```
(set-option :produce-models true)
(set-logic QF_FP)
(define-fun zero () (_ FloatingPoint 11 53) (_ +zero 11 53))
(declare-fun s0 () (_ FloatingPoint 11 53))

(get-value (s0))

Z3 responds:

```
sat
((s0 (_ +zero 11 53)))
```

i.e., `0` as the remainder of division of `0` by `0`. I do believe the correct result should be `NaN`

issue_title: Revamp Convolutional Layers
issue_body:
We should refactor/clean up the different convolutional layers in Lasagne:

* [ ] Refactor the existing code to have a common base class for all convolution dimensionalities and all implementations (as discussed in #498)
* [ ] Reconsider the interface(s) of the convolutional layers in light of the new `conv2d()` interface in Theano -- can `Conv2DLayer` support more than it does now, and should we drop some of the implementation-specific layers?
* [ ] Add a `Deconv2DLayer`. Theano provides a placeholder Op for the gradient wrt. input now, so we can provide a more direct route than via the `InverseLayer`.

(Side issue: At least the first step would be interesting for pooling as well.)

issue_title: Regularization
issue_body:
We should implement some commonly used regularizers (L1, L2, sparsity penalties on the activations as in sparse autoencoders, ...).

How should we do this? The `nntools.regularization` module I included in the initial commit was an afterthought and should be treated as more of a placeholder.

In #11 @f0k already mentioned that it's probably a good idea to make the regularization module operate on Theano expressions, not `Layer` instances, so that it can be used in isolation.

Any ideas? We should also take into account that some regularizers operate on model parameters (e.g. L1, L2) and others operate on activations (autoencoder sparsity penalty) and are data-dependent.

issue_body:
pyfilesystem2 requires six version `1.10.*` in `setup.py`:

Is this a hard requirement? Version 1.11.0 of six has just been released and we've been hitting the following error ever since, when setting up our testing infrastructure:

```python

issue_title: Request support for foreign key id fields
issue_body:
Would it be possible to support foreign key id fields? What I mean by that is the ability to use `author_id` like this:

```
class BookResource(resources.ModelResource):

    class Meta:
        model = Book
        fields = ('id', 'name', 'author_id', 'price',)
```

The advantage over using `author__id` is that an export does not require an additional join or query to the `Author` table to get the author id. 

I was able to work around this by overriding `get_export_queryset()` and setting up the resources like this:

```
class BookResource(resources.ModelResource):

    class Meta:
        model = Book
        fields = ('id', 'name', 'author', 'price',)

    def_dehydrate_author(self, book):
        return book.author_id
```

But it would be nice to have a more straightforward way to express this.

It looks like the relevant code lives in `resources.py`, but I'm not sure what exactly needs to change to support this, or if there is a reason I haven't seen why this would be a bad idea.

issue_title: Running the scripts thru Mavproxy
issue_body:
Hi, I have a question about the compatibility of dronekit-python 2.0 with Mavproxy.
In our project, the pixhawk flight controller is used with a odroid-c2 companion computer. The connection between the pixhawk and the odroid is set up thru serial and between the ground station(PC) and the odroid is set up thru telemetry. The overall connection interface we are using is Mavproxy. 
So while the drone is in the air, we don't have access to the shell of the odroid, and, therefore, could not simply run the python script in the linux terminal. 
Hence, I was wondering whether there is way to start the dronekit-python api and run a specific script located on the odroid in the Mavproxy interface? As I read, this was possible in the older version (1.5), but in the update to 2.0, the dependency on Mavproxy was relaxed.

The KD tree code [manipulates the mask](https://github.com/pytroll/pyresample/blob/281549ccf9d82fa23d9b57a63283d186a43d29b2/pyresample/kd_tree.py#L720), which raises a [numpy warning](https://github.com/numpy/numpy/blob/master/doc/release/1.11.0-notes.rst#futurewarnings).

To replicate:
```python
from satpy.scene import Scene
global_scene = Scene(platform_name = "SNPP", sensor = "viirs", 
    base_dir = "viirs_test_download_2017-08-25_ND")
global_scene.load(["M10"])
global_scene.resample("worldeqc3km70")
```

I'm using numpy 1.13.1, satpy 0.7.1 and pyresample 1.5.0.

issue_title: add a dependency on eventually
issue_body: Since there's a bunch of stuff I am proposing removing ( #24 #25 26 ) we should probably have a way of emitting some warnings and generally tracking when things need to be removed.   https://pypi.python.org/pypi/eventually has some functionality that can do that.  (This issue doesn't really stand on its own; we don't need it until we've got agreement & implementation on one of those other issues.)

issue_title: If-Match: * does not match the spec
issue_body: Per https://github.com/Kinto/kinto/pull/1064#issuecomment-279351031, the behavior of `If-Match: *` is the opposite of `If-None-Match: *`. `If-None-Match: *` should succeed if no resource exists at the given URL and fail if any resource exists at the given URL, but `If-Match: *` should fail if no resource exists at the given URL and succeed if any resource exists at the given URL. Currently `If-Match: *` does nothing (it always succeeds).

issue_title: Throwing an exception in ResourceChanged fails the entire batch?
issue_body:
I tried writing a plugin like this:

```
from pyramid import httpexceptions

from kinto.core.events import ACTIONS, ResourceChanged


def maybe_block_delete(event):
    for change in event.impacted_records:
        collection = change['old']
        if collection['id'] == 'storage-sync-crypto':
            raise httpexceptions.HTTPMethodNotAllowed("You may not delete the storage-sync-crypto collection.")


def includeme(config):
    # Redirect default to the right endpoint
    config.add_subscriber(maybe_block_delete, ResourceChanged,
                          for_resources=('collection',),
                          for_actions=(ACTIONS.DELETE,))

    config.add_api_capability(
        "dont_delete_crypto",
        description="Try to prevent deletion of storage-sync-crypto collections.",
        url="https://bugzilla.mozilla.org/show_bug.cgi?id=1359879")
```

I tried to test it like this:

```
http -v -j --auth 'user:pass2' POST localhost:8888/v1/batch 'requests:=[{"method": "PUT", "path": "/buckets/new-bucket/collections/my-collection-2"}, {"method":"DELETE", "path":"/buckets/default/collections/my-collection"}, {"method": "DELETE", "path": "/buckets/default/collections/storage-sync-crypto"}]'
```

I got:

```
HTTP/1.1 405 Method Not Allowed
Access-Control-Expose-Headers: Backoff, Content-Length, Retry-After, Alert
Content-Length: 102
Content-Type: application/json
Date: Mon, 01 May 2017 22:16:38 GMT
Server: waitress
X-Content-Type-Options: nosniff

{
    "code": 405,
    "errno": 115,
    "error": "Method Not Allowed",
    "message": "Method not allowed on this endpoint."
}
```

Is this the correct behavior? I assumed that only the one request would fail, similarly to how it would if I had put a nonmatching If-Match on a single request. On the other hand, maybe the entire batch has to fail because I'm rejecting the transaction?

issue_title: Autocomplete assumes integer pk's
issue_body:
Python==3.6.1
Django==1.11.4
django-jet==1.0.6

We use django.utils.crypto.get_random_string values for all our PK's so we can use them in url's etc without being concerned about someone walking them.
However as currently implemented Jet's autocomplete assumes id's are integers and silently fails if they are not.
This is due to this line:
https://github.com/geex-arts/django-jet/blob/dev/jet/forms.py#L101

Changing it to a CharField seems to resolve the matter.

issue_title: Random execution exceptions
issue_body:
| Required Info  |   |
|---|---|
| Camera Model | SR300 & D435 | 
| Firmware Version | 3.21.0.0 & 5.08.15 | 
| Operating System & Version | Win10 Pro version 1709 build 16299.19  |
| SDK Version | 2.9.0 |

Using VS2017, C++, COM DLLs, and threads, we have implemented the library. We are experiencing random execution exception crashes of 2 kinds (1) at the Windows message loop and (2) at a unknown code location (there is no debug info, even though all projects have PDB files and debug info).  This is a legacy robot program that used to use GigE cameras, so we know our code is very stable. These exceptions make no sense especially at the Windows message loop. How can the message loop be running fine, then suddenly, the message loop doesn't have execution access? Could something be changing the page tables? We have no code that does that. Only standard C++ and WinAPI.

issue_title: Please support "rs2_set_region_of_interest" and "rs2_get_region_of_interest" API calls in the .NET wrapper
issue_body:
| Required Info                         |                                                                |
|---------------------------------|------------------------------------------- |
| Camera Model                       | D415/D435 | 
| Firmware Version                   | 05.09.13.00 | 
| Operating System & Version | Win 10 | 
| Platform                                 | PC|
| SDK Version                            |  2.14                          | 
| Language                            |  C# | 

### Issue Description
I would like to be able to use the "rs2_set_region_of_interest" and "rs2_get_region_of_interest" API calls against a sensor from the .NET wrapper. I can see that the calls are defined in NativeMethods.cs, however they are not referenced from the Sensor class (or any others).

What I am trying to achieve is to be able to tell the camera the area of the picture that I am actually interested in so that where there are objects at different distances the area I define is the area with the best focus. I have not seen any other way to achieve this within the SDK and if I am approaching it incorrectly then please let me know.

Alternatively, if I were to try and update the wrapper myself (I think I can see what would be required), would this be acceptable - subject to standards etc. - as a pull request?

issue_title: Realsense ROS node stops publishing images
issue_body:
| Required Info                         |                                                                |
|---------------------------------|------------------------------------------- |
| Camera Model                       | D415, D435
| Firmware Version                   | 2.10.2
| Operating System & Version |  Linux (Ubuntu 16.04) | 
| Kernel Version (Linux Only)    |  4.13.0-38-generic, 4.13.0-36-generic | 
| Platform                                 | PC  |
| SDK Version                            |  2.10.2                          | 


### Issue Description
realsense ROS node stops publishing color images (didn't test with depth) on topic.

## Steps to reproduce
1. Start a realsense ROS node (as described in tutorial) publish color images on e.g. /camera1/image_raw
2. Start reading from the topic using e.g. `rostopic echo --noarr /camera1/image_raw`
3. Put your CPU under heavy pressure, by e.g. using ./cpuburn 

Now if you wait a couple of minutes, you will see at some point that there no new messages published on the topic.

Generally, it seems that the more realsense ROS nodes are on, the faster this phenomena occurs. 
The bigger CPU pressure, the faster this phenomena will occur. (I have 4 cores with hyperthreading, I usually put ./cpuburn -n 16)
It seems that this phenomena occurs faster if you keep subscribing to the topic:
`while [ 1 ]; do timeout 1 rostopic echo --noarr /camera1/image_raw`

I couldn't reproduce this on realsense-viewer.
The same phenomena occurs even if I set export OMP_NUM_THREADS=1
The same happens when instead of `rostopic echo` one uses python's rospy library.

issue_title: As a user, I can publish a particular repository version.
issue_body:
Currently, users publish by supplying `repository` to the publication endpoint. This new feature allows them to specify `repository_version` instead of the latest version.

1. Create a repo with at least 2 repository versions (e.g. versions 1 and 2).
2. Create a publication by supplying the non-latest `repository_version` (ie version 1).
3. With the new publication confirm that it contains content from version 1 and repository_version attribute on the publication points to version 1 (and not version 2).

issue_title: Speeding up Rendering
issue_body:
Is there a good way to render MuJoCo images (i.e an image of the environment) fast / What's the fastest way to render?

I am trying to get a speed up in rendering (goal is to collect more data) by spawning multiple environments, but when I test a single environment worker and multiple environment workers, I see a performance decrease in speed.

```python

import time
st = time.time()
env = gym.make(env_id)
env.reset()
for i in range(3000):
    action = env.action_space.sample()
    obs, rewards, dones, info = env.step(action)
    env.render(mode='rgb_array')
    if dones:
        env.reset()

print(time.time() - st)
```

Gives me about 15s to render 3000 frames, where as an MPI enabled version, (3 MPI workers, using `SubprocVecEnv`) is taking longer (~24s to render the same 3000 frames).

Is there any way I can boost the speed at which frames get rendered?

issue_title: Support for dynamics modding and randomization (DynamicsModder)
issue_body:
In [*Sim-to-Real Transfer of Robotic Control with Dynamics Randomization*](https://arxiv.org/pdf/1710.06537.pdf), they randomize model properties of the Mujoco simulation like mass of links and damping of joints.

Is there any planned support for this dynamics modding and randomization as there is for texture modding ([TextureModder](https://github.com/openai/mujoco-py/blob/master/mujoco_py/modder.py#L198-L431))?

I am guessing dynamics modding is not as straightforward, due to how Mujoco handles model loading.

It looks like the [current workaround](https://github.com/openai/gym/issues/234) is altering an xml file and reloading the model from that.  If this is the best way, would it make sense to add a nicer API for it?

issue_title: Django: Map Form to Mutation
issue_body:
Currently it's possible to map Django models to `ObjectType`s using `DjangoNode`. I think it would be nice to have a similar feature that allows mapping forms to `Mutation` with a `DjangoMutation` class.

This would provide several great improvements:
- No need to duplicate form fields in `Input` classes anymore.
- There would be a simple and obvious way to handle validation.
- The code could map `form.errors` to `info.context.errors` (It took a while until I figured out how to do that) on it's own.

In the simple case you'd only have to define the outputs and a method that takes the validated data and returns an instance of the mutation class. I believe that even that would be unnecessary, if you're working with `ModelForm`s. In that case a default method could just save the form and return the model instance wrapped in the corresponding `DjangoNode`.

Code that gets us some of the way to such a class already appears to exist, which was written for the django-filter integration.

I'm not that familiar with the code base itself but I have read quite a bit of it and I' think it might be able to implement this. I'm interested to know what you think of it and whether this has a chance to be accepted.

issue_title: Exception logging
issue_body:
Hi! I'm finally catching up to upgrading to graphene 2.0. A lot of code churn, but there are some nice improvements.

My main complaint at the moment is the noisy logging of graphql errors on execution. I can't find what is logging this to work out how to disable it. Especially in a test suite which is designed to catch errors and verify the messages they have raised, printing a traceback on every test is very unhelpful! I have logging disabled to `CRITICAL` level when running my test suite.

I'm not sure if this is an issue in Graphene, Graphql core, or graphene-django but I can't find any obvious logging config or stdout writing in any of the projects which would cause it.

issue_title: `order_by` clause issue with django & graphql
issue_body:
Hi,

When using the `order_by` clause in graphql,
- the graphql representation is correctly `orderBy` (camelCase).
- the value however is expected in snake case, which does not match the graphql auto generated code (it should match the API exposed field in all logic).

I tried to fix this but there are 2 places I could find where the order by is injected in a djgango qs:

1. In
`graphene.contrib.django.filter.fields.DjangoFilterConnectionField`
```
    def get_order(self, args):
        return args.get('order_by', None)
```

Which does not tackle the snake case at all but at least the return is correctly handled by (in case of `None` (same file):
```
    def get_queryset(self, qs, args, info):
        filterset_class = self.filterset_class
        filter_kwargs = self.get_filter_kwargs(args)
        order = self.get_order(args)
        if order:
            qs = qs.order_by(order)
        unknown = filterset_class(data=filter_kwargs, queryset=qs)
        return unknown
```

This in *also* (not sure why, maybe I did something wrong) pushed to:
`django_filters.filterset.BaseFilterSet`
```
    @property
    def qs(self):
        if not hasattr(self, '_qs'):
            valid = self.is_bound and self.form.is_valid()

            if self.strict and self.is_bound and not valid:
                self._qs = self.queryset.none()
                return self._qs

            # start with all the results and filter from there
            qs = self.queryset.all()
            for name, filter_ in six.iteritems(self.filters):
                value = None
                if valid:
                    value = self.form.cleaned_data[name]
                else:
                    raw_value = self.form[name].value()
                    try:
                        value = self.form.fields[name].clean(raw_value)
                    except forms.ValidationError:
                        # for invalid values either:
                        # strictly "apply" filter yielding no results and get outta here
                        if self.strict:
                            self._qs = self.queryset.none()
                            return self._qs
                        else:  # or ignore this filter altogether
                            pass

                if value is not None:  # valid & clean data
                    qs = filter_.filter(qs, value)

            if self._meta.order_by:
                order_field = self.form.fields[self.order_by_field]
                data = self.form[self.order_by_field].data
                ordered_value = None
                try:
                    ordered_value = order_field.clean(data)
                except forms.ValidationError:
                    pass

                if ordered_value in EMPTY_VALUES and self.strict:
                    ordered_value = self.form.fields[self.order_by_field].choices[0][0]

                if ordered_value:
                    qs = qs.order_by(*self.get_order_by(ordered_value))

            self._qs = qs

        return self._qs
```
Which does not handle at all the `None` return for `get_order_by`.

3. When I tested this, I got it not to crash turning the camelCase to snake case in the `get_order` method, however after that, while the qs was correct (I printed it out) the return from the API was systematically empty... (in that case #2 did not seem to kick in)
4. When I tested sorting by a composite field (field that I had defined on the `Node` manually and not part of the django model) is when I hit #1 and #2 here. I could not get this working at all.
5. When the system finds an `order_by` it does not know: it prints the django error which exposes *all* the fields of the raw django model to the API: not super secure... It seems like the system does not validate the `order_by` "clause" / arguments before sending it to django.

This is what I found after a couple days of debugging and trying to go around the issue. Please let me know if I can help in any way. The doc was very light on the subject and I understand from slack that @syrusakbary has code changes.

I did not know the best way to help you/us resolve this. Thank you.

issue_body:
* safety version: 1.1.1
* Python version: 2.7
* Operating System: RHEL7

### Description

Whenever **safety** stumbles over unpinned requirements, it fails with an exception:

This is the result:

```
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/opt/virtualenv/safety/bin/safety", line 11, in <module>
    sys.exit(cli())
  File "/opt/virtualenv/safety/lib/python2.7/site-packages/click/core.py", line 722, in __call__
    return self.main(*args, **kwargs)
  File "/opt/virtualenv/safety/lib/python2.7/site-packages/click/core.py", line 697, in main
    rv = self.invoke(ctx)
  File "/opt/virtualenv/safety/lib/python2.7/site-packages/click/core.py", line 1066, in invoke
    return _process_result(sub_ctx.command.invoke(sub_ctx))
  File "/opt/virtualenv/safety/lib/python2.7/site-packages/click/core.py", line 895, in invoke
    return ctx.invoke(self.callback, **ctx.params)
  File "/opt/virtualenv/safety/lib/python2.7/site-packages/click/core.py", line 535, in invoke
    return callback(*args, **kwargs)
  File "/opt/virtualenv/safety/lib/python2.7/site-packages/safety/cli.py", line 40, in check
    vulns = safety.check(packages=packages, key=key, db_mirror=db, cached=cache)
  File "/opt/virtualenv/safety/lib/python2.7/site-packages/safety/safety.py", line 130, in check
    for pkg in packages:
  File "/opt/virtualenv/safety/lib/python2.7/site-packages/safety/util.py", line 81, in read_requirements
    req=req.name),
AttributeError: Requirement instance has no attribute 'name'
```

This happens both with unpinned versions in `requirements.txt` and with unpinned versions in the `pip freeze` output (e.g. git snapshots).

In my setup the parsed requirement `req`, which is accessed on line 81 in `utils.py` only has the following attributes:

```
['specifier', 'project_name', 'unsafe_name', 'key', 'hashCmp', '_Requirement__hash', 'specs', 'extras']
```

It looks like you are trying to access the `name` attribute of a `Requirement`, which should not be present according to [setuptool's documentation](http://setuptools.readthedocs.io/en/latest/pkg_resources.html#requirement-methods-and-attributes]).

issue_title: CoAP - Duplicate message detection & Commissioning Problems
issue_body:
This is the embedded side of:  https://github.com/openthread/borderrouter/issues/81

At a general level, the public CoAP API should provide a means to detect duplicate CoAP requests of a server,  this currently does not exist.

At a specific level, in the Leader when a commissioning petition is received there is no 'duplicate message detection' - there needs to be.

For example, this time line:

1) Using the Android commissioning App as an example - it sends a Commission Petition.
2) The otbr-agent (on the e border router) receives a commissioning petition.
3) This is forwarded to the NCP, which then forwards to the current LEADER

Note: the key variable here is: 'mSessionId", this is the commissioning session id.
As a concrete example, we'll use the mSessionId = 42

4) The leader grants the request and sends back a reply, with SessionID = 42

See: https://github.com/openthread/openthread/blob/master/src/core/meshcop/leader.cpp#L102

5) HOWEVER - if the Android app sends a DUPLICATE petition (and it does)
6) The leader receives the DUPLICATE petition - there is no duplicate detection

7) The Leader code does "++mSessionId" and generates a new session id, and sends the response.
The Leader now has mSessionID = 43

8) Later - (about 20 to 30 seconds later) - the Android App sends KEEP ALIVE, the android app however sends back the FIRST session ID  ie:  ID=42, however the Leader has "43"

Then here (below) when the KeepAlive is managed the session IDs do not match..
   https://github.com/openthread/openthread/blob/master/src/core/meshcop/leader.cpp#L189

And as a result a "REJECT" message is sent to the Android App.

9) Sadly - there is a 3rd bug - in the Android App - it seems to ignore the REJECT message and continues to say that it is trying to find the device ... The android app sits there ... displaying searching.... endlessly, meanwhile every 30 seconds the Commissioner sends REJECT in response to keep alive messages.

issue_title: pyfftw.FFTW needs a function __getnewargs__ for pickle
issue_body:
In order to use pyfftw.FFTW with multiprocessing on Windows, the fft plans have to be pickled and unpickled.

Now unpickling a plan leads to an error:

```
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "bug_pyfftw_pickle.py", line 31, in <module>
    fftplan = pickle.load(f)
  File "/usr/lib/python2.7/pickle.py", line 1384, in load
    return Unpickler(file).load()
  File "/usr/lib/python2.7/pickle.py", line 864, in load
    dispatch[key](self)
  File "/usr/lib/python2.7/pickle.py", line 1089, in load_newobj
    obj = cls.__new__(cls, *args)
  File "pyfftw.pyx", line 694, in pyfftw.pyfftw.FFTW.__cinit__ (/tmp/pip_build_root/pyfftw/pyfftw/pyfftw.c:4929)
TypeError: __cinit__() takes at least 2 positional arguments (0 given)

```

It may be possible to solve this issue simply by adding a function `__getnewargs__` to the class in the pyx file.

Simple example showing the issue and the idea to solve it:
```
import pickle

import pyfftw

ny = nx = 8
shapeX = [ny, nx]
shapeK = [ny, nx//2 + 1]
arrayX = pyfftw.n_byte_align_empty(shapeX, 16, 'float32')
arrayK = pyfftw.n_byte_align_empty(shapeK, 16, 'complex64')

error = True

if error:
    FFTW = pyfftw.FFTW
else:
    class FFTW(pyfftw.FFTW):
        def __getnewargs__(self):
            print('__getnewargs__')
            return (self.get_input_array(), self.get_output_array())
    
fftplan = FFTW(
    input_array=arrayX, output_array=arrayK,
    axes=(0, 1), direction='FFTW_FORWARD')

name = 'tmp.pickle'

with open(name, 'wb') as f:
    pickle.dump(fftplan, f, pickle.HIGHEST_PROTOCOL)
        
with open(name, 'rb') as f:
    fftplan = pickle.load(f)

```

issue_title: dd.read_parquet("gc:") read data from gcs fails
issue_body:
I created a Kubernetes cluster on google cloud and deployed dask using helm from stable/dask chart. The Jupyter Notebook provides with the examples folder does not work as gcsfs package is not included.

So I tried to do my own way. Start a local Jupyter Notebook and connect to the dask cluster in the google cloud.

```python
import dask
from dask.distributed import Client, progress
client = Client("IP:8786")
client
```
Works perfectly.

To avoid access problems is initialize gcsfs beforehand:
```python
import gcsfs
gcs = gcsfs.GCSFileSystem(project='NAME', token=r"PATH")
```

Also works and I can `ls` the files.

Then I want to read the parquet:
```python
import dask.dataframe as dd
df = dd.read_parquet('gs://anaconda-public-data/nyc-taxi/nyc.parquet')
df = df.persist()
progress(df)
```
This fails with error:
```
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/opt/conda/lib/python3.6/site-packages/distributed/protocol/pickle.py", line 59, in loads
    return pickle.loads(x)
ModuleNotFoundError: No module named 'urllib3'
distributed.worker - WARNING - Could not deserialize task
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/opt/conda/lib/python3.6/site-packages/distributed/worker.py", line 1266, in add_task
    self.tasks[key] = _deserialize(function, args, kwargs, task)
  File "/opt/conda/lib/python3.6/site-packages/distributed/worker.py", line 644, in _deserialize
    args = pickle.loads(args)
  File "/opt/conda/lib/python3.6/site-packages/distributed/protocol/pickle.py", line 59, in loads
    return pickle.loads(x)
ModuleNotFoundError: No module named 'urllib3'
```
However `urllib3` should be installed. In order to read the parquet and provide snappy I had to edit a `config.yaml` as following:

```yaml
# config.yaml
scheduler:
  replicas: 1
  env:
    - name: EXTRA_CONDA_PACKAGES
      value: numba xarray fastparquet pyarrow snappy python-snappy gcsfs s3fs dask-ml urllib3 -c conda-forge
    - name: EXTRA_PIP_PACKAGES
      value: urllib3
worker:
  replicas: 8

    limits:
      cpu: 2
      memory: 7.5G

    - name: EXTRA_CONDA_PACKAGES

# We want to keep the same packages on the worker and jupyter environments

    - name: EXTRA_CONDA_PACKAGES

issue_title: Refactor shell command to use cmd2 stdout
issue_body:
Currently the ``shell`` command (or ``!`` shortcut) is implemented using ``os.system()``.  This doesn't allows passing the cmd2.stdout to the other process and thus makes unit testsing anything related to a shell command more difficult.

Consider refactoring ``shell`` so it is implemented using something like ``subprocess.Popen()`` where we can pass it a stdout to assist with easier unit testing and such.
