issue_title: Make a 2.2 release
issue_body:
We have a decent amount of new features and bug fixes for a new stable v2.2 release.  Some big features (embedding support seems the biggest), lots of minor ones, and various bug fixes have landed into `master` over the last four months.  I'm not suggesting we release it tomorrow, but I think we should start thinking about where to draw the line for 2.2.

PRs I think we should aim to merge:

- #951, #952 are bug fixes (and ready to merge).
- #805 (init factories) - the implementation is done; we need to decide whether to make this core (i.e. included by `pybind11.h` or not), and then I'll update the documentation accordingly before merging.
- #949 (`py::local` support for keeping a type's registration local to the module).  This was a sort of experiment to think about fixing #919, but I think it worked out into a nice feature -- and I was actually surprised just how little code it actually needed to implement.  This still needs me to write up the documentation, but I'll do that in the next couple of days.
- #934 (constexpr type signatures for C++11 and MSVC).  Currently has a minor merge conflict, but aside from that I think it's ready to merge.
- #928 (FindPythonLibs{,New} cmake compatibility) - this is ready to merge AFAICT.
- #925 (bool and numpy bool conversions)
- #901 (cutting off the maximum length in generated signatures) is a nice cosmetic feature.  It needs some more tweaking, but if that happens it seems worth including.

Other features in the queue that I think might not make it:

- #864 (Revising type casters) - I think this is going to be a pretty significant change, and likely to go through a fair amount of discussion/suggestions/changes from start to finish.  I'm not opposed to including it if its done soon, but of course I also think it's important to get it right.
- #781 (python 3.4 enums) - this is still marked WIP and, I guess, still in progress.  I think the last discussion was that it was going to be separate from `enum_<>` due to backwards-compatibility issues with how `enum_` worked; beyond that I'm not entirely sure of the status.


Thoughts?

issue_title: GKE authentication
issue_body:
pykube is not capable of authenticating against GKE clusters. Recent clusters on GKE use Google OAuth to authenticate. The user configuration in `~/.kube/config` looks like:

```
user:
  auth-provider:
    name: gcp
```

Once `kubectl` runs with that configuration it adds in the `config` key with an `access-token` and `expiry`.

In my initial research, I learned that oauth2client uses httplib which is a non-starter for integrating it with pykube. However, there's hope with https://github.com/google/oauth2client/issues/128

issue_body:
Currently we have:
```
certbot certificates
Saving debug log to /var/log/letsencrypt/letsencrypt.log

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Found the following certs:
  Certificate Name: isnot.org
    Domains: isnot.org goxogle.com is.isnot.org www.isnot.org
    Valid Until: 2017-01-25 22:06:00+00:00 (55 days)
    Certificate Path: /etc/letsencrypt/live/isnot.org/fullchain.pem
    Private Key Path: /etc/letsencrypt/live/isnot.org/privkey.pem
  Certificate Name: goxogle.com
    Domains: goxogle.com www.isnot.org
    Valid Until: 2016-04-07 23:39:00+00:00 (EXPIRED)
    Certificate Path: /etc/letsencrypt/live/goxogle.com/fullchain.pem
    Private Key Path: /etc/letsencrypt/live/goxogle.com/privkey.pem
  Certificate Name: demo.reworld.org
    Domains: demo.reworld.org
    Valid Until: 2017-01-02 21:23:00+00:00 (32 days)
    Certificate Path: /etc/letsencrypt/live/demo.reworld.org/fullchain.pem
    Private Key Path: /etc/letsencrypt/live/demo.reworld.org/privkey.pem
  Certificate Name: isnot.org-0001
    Domains: isnot.org
    Valid Until: 2017-01-02 21:23:00+00:00 (32 days)
    Certificate Path: /etc/letsencrypt/live/isnot.org-0001/fullchain.pem
    Private Key Path: /etc/letsencrypt/live/isnot.org-0001/privkey.pem
------------------------------------------------------------------------------
```

We should probably support the following things: 
* easy grepping to see if a cert is valid or not
* future addition of OCSP checks for invalidity due to revocation
* marking whether a cert is a test / staging cert!
* change the "Valid Until" string to "Expiry Date", because it's confusing if an invalid/expired cert has a "Valid Until" listing, and someone might foolishly grep for "Valid", having never seen what an expired cert looks like.

issue_title: certbot/cli.py is too long
issue_body:
Probably the easiest approach is to put `HelpfulArgumentParser` and related classes/methods into their own file. We can then use the class in `cli.py`, but it doesn't have to also be defined there.

This also dramatically simplifies unit testing `HelpfulArgumentParser`.

issue_body: [This Paper](https://arxiv.org/pdf/1403.0297.pdf)  offers a promising algorithm for protecting HTTPS against traffic analysis attacks. It would be an interesting research project to figure out whether we could implement this in existing Nginx or Apache configuration files, and offer it (or if not, what it would take to offer it to our users).

issue_title: Convertion of detection TLET and DLET with --converter plotdata
issue_body:
I have found a problem with the bdo2txt.py converter file, when converting a bdo-file with a single region of detection and using the converter: --converter plotdata 
When the scoring is done with TLET or DLET I get 30 results in the .txt.dat file instead of just one result. For all other scorers like energy, dose and so on, even for DLETG and TLETG it works fine and only a single result gets out.
It should be mentioned that 30 is exactly the number of jobs I ran in parallel to get the bdo-files in the first place, so this may explain why there are 30 entries in the file. But the mean value does not correspond to the real value I get when I use the --converter standard

Further, for TLETG and DLETG the results with both types of converter options seem to giving a scaling factor. The results are scaled down by a factor of 10^5 which is the number of primary particles in a single job (out of the 30 parallel jobs).

Kind regards Vicki Taasti



[Converter_problem.zip](https://github.com/DataMedSci/pymchelper/files/327965/Converter_problem.zip)

issue_title: UnauthorizedRegistration
issue_body:
Im trying to use pywebpush under python 2.7 (and 3.5) without any success. I am getting the error `UnauthorizedRegistration` with a `400` response code. Using the [Push Companion](https://web-push-codelab.appspot.com/), everything works just fine (with the same key and same subscription info). Also, my internal clock is synced (some people reported having issues if your clock is not synced). My code : ```python from pywebpush import WebPusher import json sub = json.loads('{"endpoint":"https://fcm.googleapis.com/fcm/send/","keys":{"p256dh":"","auth":""}}') r = WebPusher(sub).send(data='hey') ``` Which returns : ``` > r.text u'\n\nUnauthorizedRegistration\n\n\n

UnauthorizedRegistration

\n

Error 400

\n\n\n' > r.status_code 400 ``` I am getting the same result no matter which Python version I use. Any help debugging this would be really **really** appreciated...

issue_title: SPC inspector producing graphs from .spc files
Installs broken pathlib backport, breaks python3 pathlib usage by all other modules
issue_body:
pathlib-1.0.1 backport required by thinc is no longer maintained and breaks other modules that depend on the correct pathlib.

See https://pypi.org/project/pathlib/

issue_title: Automatic to_host and from_host of _input, _depends, and _output
issue_body: This looks like a logical thing to do and would make the interface a bit simpler. The `from_host` is needed for the particular example because we would like to retrieve more than only the `chrName.txt`, but the whole index.

issue_title: Automatic preview of remote targets.
issue_body: With the new `-r HOST` option, we could preview specific files on remote host. This however does not apply to automatic preview of sos steps. We should therefore automatically add `-r` option for automatic preview.

issue_title: explicit path map / rename with options to_host and from_host
issue_body:
I have a need to copy data from remote host to local host under another name. This is currently done with `from_host` and then a bunch of rename operations. We might be able to help under this situation by allowing something like

```
to_host={'local_file': 'remote_file'},
from_host={'remote_file': 'local_file'}
```
in addition to 

```
to_host='local_file', from_host='loca_file'
```
The underlying explanation is that SoS would not translate paths with an explicit path map.

issue_title: Stop checking readonly global variables
issue_body:
Checking if global variables are changed has unexpected performance effect and it triggers problems with simple statements such as

```
name = 'default'

issue_title: Remove expand option from action after parsing

would fail because `len` does not take keyword parameter `expand`.

issue_title: Split out CLI into separate repo
issue_labels: [{'id': 429586299, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/zhmcclient/python-zhmcclient/labels/area:%20code', 'name': 'area: code', 'color': '207de5', 'default': False}, {'id': 429588767, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/zhmcclient/python-zhmcclient/labels/resolution:%20fixed', 'name': 'resolution: fixed', 'color': 'cccccc', 'default': False}, {'id': 533151901, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/zhmcclient/python-zhmcclient/labels/type:%20cleanup', 'name': 'type: cleanup', 'color': '84b6eb', 'default': False}]
issue_body:
The zhmc CLI is currently part of the Python package `zhmcclient`. This drives some unnecessary package dependencies for users of the zhmcclient API. Also, conceptually, the CLI is on top of the public zhmcclient API and should not use any internal interfaces.

The proposal is to split out the CLi part into a separate Git repo and Pypi package.

This is an incompatible change for users of the zhmcclient package because that package looses the CLI which would be in a separate package.

issue_title: Create an official, Automated Build image on Docker Hub
issue_body:
Docker Hub allows you to create Automated Builds from source: https://docs.docker.com/docker-hub/builds/
It would add another packaging/distribution/installation method, whose buildings would be triggered automatically on each commit. It also allows to create different image tags from git tags & branches (thus allowing to test ```:develop``` right away, [as shown](https://hub.docker.com/r/pataquets/mail-parser-src/builds/)).
Also, documentation could easily include a canonical `docker run` statement to quickly try the tool with just a single command.

By making the image build via an AB, you give the resulting image verifiability and auditability. Also, the build is fully automatic. You can have the latest image tag build from HEAD and individual image tags from git's release tags.
Some people avoid non-verifiable (manually uploaded) images due to security & traceability reasons. Docker search command clearly displays AB when listing images.

Just a free Docker Hub account and a quick setup would do. Ping me if you need help.

issue_title: Python3 2x slower than Python2 for some operations
issue_body:
<!--
General questions should be asked on the mailing list ray-dev@googlegroups.com.

Before submitting an issue, please fill out the following form.
-->

### System information
- **OS Platform and Distribution (e.g., Linux Ubuntu 16.04)**: OS X
- **Ray installed from (source or binary)**: source
- **Python version**: Python 2.7 & Python 3.6

### Describe the problem
I am seeing a slowdown of 2x when I run things on Python3 vs Python2. The slowdown doesn't look to be linked to creating actors, but rather in executing certain types of tasks.

Code to reproduce is below.
`python2 time: 4.214621067047119`
`python3 time: 9.676268259005155`

### Source code / logs

```python
import ray
ray.init()

@ray.remote
class Actor(object):
    def __init__(self, rows={"Item1": "Value1", "Item2": "Value2"}):
        self.rows = rows
    def split(self):
        half = int(len(self.rows)/2)
        items = list(self.rows.items())
        items.sort()
        second_dict = dict(items[half:])
        self.rows = dict(items[:half])
        return second_dict
    def get_rows(self):
        return self.rows

def f():
    actor = Actor.remote()
    actor2 = Actor.remote(actor.split.remote())
    print(ray.get(actor2.get_rows.remote()))
    print(ray.get(actor.get_rows.remote()))

import timeit
timeit.timeit(f, number=10)
```

issue_title: Scheduling based on labels
issue_body:
Maybe scheduling could also work on custom labels. For example, I could run a worker saying that it runs on a machine with `datasets_available` and then I could have a job which would require that it is scheduled only on a machine/worker with `datasets_available` label.

So initially we thought of using a distributed file system mounted on every worker's machine and then have workers access datasets as needed. But that would require scheduling to know if a dataset is already moved to a worker by the distributed file system or not (to schedule jobs close to data, if possible). But I think it could be simpler if only one machine has datasets and then jobs read it as needed and store them into object store and then Ray can transport those objects around as needed. We are working anyway with datasets which can fit into memory, so it seems this is the best approach anyway. And if we get to the phase of larger datasets, locally serializing them from object store to a cache on local drive seems even much better than whole dataset parsing from scratch. (Although, it depends on compression; uncompressed images can be much larger than compressed ones.)

issue_title: run_command is slower than paramiko
issue_body:
I'm not sure anybody is looking at the google group anymore, so I'll try to move this here to github.

I have some existing code using paramiko and was doing some tests to replace it. Unfortunately the round trip time of individual commands seems to be quite a bit slower using pssh.
My test code can be found here: https://gist.github.com/mbarkhau/e4f3dde91bbb137b29973c362e2c128e

These are the results

```
                      min [ms]    max [ms]    avg [ms]
paramiko                  5.60       10.73        7.46
pssh run_command         24.18       69.31       56.47
pssh execute             54.36       59.10       56.12
     open                40.79       43.24       42.19
     cmd                 13.37       15.86       13.92
```

It looks like the command execution itself is comparable to paramiko, but I haven't found a way to use the API without doing open_session for every single command.

Is there a different way to use the libraries (maybe ssh2-python directly) which would have comparable/better RTT vs paramiko? Maybe this issue can be resolved with improvements to documentation.

I occasionally receive [requests](https://github.com/pbugnion/gmaps/issues/62#issuecomment-294424416) for saving maps as static images. It seems like this could be done with a tool like [html2canvas](http://html2canvas.hertzen.com): the entire div containing the widget could just be exported to to a png. I note that something along those lines was done in [PR 314](https://github.com/jupyter-widgets/ipywidgets/pull/314), but I can't see how to use it -- apologies if I'm missing something obvious.

If the solution to this involves writing code or documentation, I'm more than happy to contribute, but I would definitely need initial guidance.

issue_title: Search route, maybe other on JSONModelObject throws AttributeError
issue_body:
Currently, calling methods on JSONModelObjects that require arguments other than all_ids to return successfully causes an AttributeError to be raised.

Triggering code:
```python
x.repositories(2).search # should be JSONRelation, throws AttributeError
```

Cause:
Currently, __getattribute__ on jsonmodels does a call with all_ids, eagerly at time of object creation.  This is fine (if extra work) for index routes.  It fails dramatically for search.

Honestly, I'm not sure what the Right Thing is to do.  There isn't a generic way to handle this "properly" (i.e. fails in __getattribute__ with an error saying that there's no route, because HEAD ALSO errors out for search.  I'm hesitant to just say "if you call something that's not on the object, you get a relation and then the relation fails if it wasn't real" because that moves the place failures happen potentially several steps down the road; you don't find out it's bad until you try to iterate through the relation.

issue_title: Should span.tags be a dict?
issue_body:
Does it make sense for {[span.tags](https://github.com/uber/jaeger-client-python/blob/master/jaeger_client/span.py#L56)} to be a dict object instead of a list? Similar to the implementation in the java client {[source](https://github.com/uber/jaeger-client-java/blob/master/jaeger-core/src/main/java/com/uber/jaeger/Span.java#L60)}

Admittedly, the behavior has been left somewhat ambiguous in [opentracing](https://github.com/opentracing/opentracing-python/blob/master/opentracing/span.py#L116), but shouldn't the Jaeger client libs maintain this degree of parity?

The CBOR format is disabled (I can see the Kinesis requests content type in `json`) and the started services are `lambda,kinesis,apigateway,sqs`.

issue_title: introduce a canonical data dir
issue_body:
Currently when we clone a new tensorlayer repo and run some tutorials, TL will download the data/weights and save them in the current directory. If we run commands like `git clean -fdx`, the data will be lost. If we have two TL repos on the same computer (not sure if this is a common case for many users), the data can't be shared.
I think we can store all the data/weight files in a directory called `DATA_DIR`, it is located at a fix path on a computer, e.g. `~/var/tensorlayer`, `~/.tensorlayer` (can be controlled by an environment variable), and we could organise the well known files as:

```
# DATA_DIR/data/<dataset-name>/<file>
# DATA_DIR/models/<model-name>/<file>
DATA_DIR/
   data/
      mnist/
         t10k-images-idx3-ubyte.gz
         ...
      cifat/
        cifar-10-binary.tar.gz
        ...
   models/
      vgg/
         vgg16_weights.npz
         vgg19_weights.npz
         ...
      yolo/
       
```
